## Who We Are

EdTrust in Texas advocates for an equitable education for Black and Latino students and students from low-income backgrounds across the state. We believe in centering the voices of Texas students and families as we work alongside them for the better future they deserve.

## Midwest

Our mission is to close the gaps in opportunity and achievement that disproportionately impact students who are the most underserved, with a particular focus on Black and Latino/a students and students from low-income backgrounds.

## New York

EdTrust–New York is a statewide education policy and advocacy organization focused first and foremost on doing right by New York’s children. Although many organizations speak up for the adults employed by schools and colleges, we advocate for students, especially those whose needs and potential are often overlooked.

## Tennessee

EdTrust-Tennessee advocates for equitable education for historically-underserved students across the state. We believe in centering the voices of Tennessee students and families as we work alongside them for the future they deserve.

## West

EdTrust–West is committed to dismantling the racial and economic barriers embedded in the California education system. Through our research and advocacy, EdTrust-West engages diverse communities dedicated to education equity and justice and increases political and public will to build an education system where students of color and multilingual learners, especially those experiencing poverty, will thrive.

## Louisiana

The Education Trust in Louisiana works to promote educational equity for historically underserved students in the Louisiana’s schools. We work alongside students, families, and communities to build urgency and collective will for educational equity and justice.

## Texas

EdTrust in Texas advocates for an equitable education for historically-underserved students across the state. We believe in centering the voices of Texas students and families as we work alongside them for the better future they deserve.

## Massachusetts

The Education Trust team in Massachusetts convenes and supports the Massachusetts Education Equity Partnership (MEEP), a collective effort of more than 20 social justice, civil rights and education organizations from across the Commonwealth working together to promote educational equity for historically underserved students in our state’s schools.

### Why STEM Equity Must Address the Experiences of Women of Color

Studies show that having a diverse academic faculty benefit students, who see themselves and their career aspirations represented, and leads to better student outcomes in STEM (science, technology, engineering, and…

![article-cropped](https://edtrust.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/article-cropped.svg)
![linkedin](https://edtrust.org/wp-content/themes/ci-uikit-block/images/ico-linkedin.svg)
![X](https://edtrust.org/wp-content/themes/ci-uikit-block/images/ico-twitter.svg)
![facebook](https://edtrust.org/wp-content/themes/ci-uikit-block/images/ico-facebook.svg)
![](https://edtrust.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/female-graduate-220x150.png)

Studies show that having a [diverse academic faculty benefit](https://edtrust.org/resource/faculty-diversity-and-student-success-go-hand-in-hand-so-why-are-university-faculties-so-white/) students, who see themselves and their career aspirations represented, and leads to better student outcomes in STEM (science, technology, engineering, and mathematics) programs. Studies repeatedly show that students have better outcomes, including persisting in STEM and graduating, when there is faculty diversity. While most faculty members and administrative leaders are White, students look to professors from diverse backgrounds as role models and mentors. As such, faculty diversity is key to student success.

The absence of faculty diversity in STEM, therefore, has broader implications, as it discourages many students of color from pursuing STEM degrees and careers. This can potentially have negative consequences for both the students themselves and society. Rapid expansion and advancements in STEM over the last several decades have led to a heightened demand for STEM professionals, who tend to [earn higher salaries](https://www.pewresearch.org/science/2021/04/01/stem-jobs-see-uneven-progress-in-increasing-gender-racial-and-ethnic-diversity/) (STEM, $77,400; non-STEM, $46,900).

Additionally, the U.S. Department of Labor projects that STEM jobs will increase by [nearly twice the rate](https://blog.dol.gov/2022/11/04/stem-day-explore-growing-careers) of other jobs in the next 10 years. Between 2010 and 2018, the number of bachelor’s degrees granted in STEM subjects [surged 62%](https://www.pewresearch.org/science/2021/04/01/stem-jobs-see-uneven-progress-in-increasing-gender-racial-and-ethnic-diversity/), which is more than three times greater than the 20% rise in all bachelor’s degrees awarded over that same period.

Yet, despite the strong interest and higher pay in STEM careers, vast disparities in attainment by race, ethnicity, and gender [persist in STEM education](https://caarpweb.org/wp-content/uploads/2014/12/Charleston-Adserias-Lang-Jackson-2014.pdf) and employment, thereby limiting access and opportunities for social and economic mobility for some — particularly women and people of color. Women, and especially women of color, also [fare worse](https://www.aauw.org/resources/research/the-stem-gap/) in STEM jobs, as many are paid less than their male counterparts. Often, women need more degrees and, consequently, take out more loans to generate an income that is comparable to that of their male colleagues. We recognize that achieving STEM equity in degree attainment and in the workplace is interconnected with other systems and structures.

Students from marginalized groups, especially women, still have less access to [AP STEM courses](https://edtrust.org/wp-content/uploads/2014/09/5Questions_Brief_FINAL_4.pdf) in high school and lower STEM degree attainment rates than their White male colleagues. Moreover, a significant number of STEM graduates of color come out of a small number of colleges and universities with diverse faculties; HBCUs, for example, produce 25% of      all Black [graduates in STEM fields](https://uncf.org/the-latest/the-impact-of-hbcus-on-diversity-in-stem-fields).

### How equitable are current STEM pathways?

Women, especially women of color, hold only a [small minority of STEM jobs](https://www.eeoc.gov/sites/default/files/2022-07/Women%20in%20STEM%20Special%20Topics%20Report%20FY%2020%20Final_1.pdf). It is also important to note that STEM degree attainment and jobs vary by field. For example, women and people of color are underrepresented in higher-paying STEM jobs, such as engineering and computing; whereas they have significantly more representation in the biological and behavioral sciences. Women are also more likely to leave STEM occupations. Black and Latino STEM students and professionals, particularly women, [routinely face harassment, workplace discrimination, and unequal pay.](https://www.pewresearch.org/social-trends/2018/01/09/women-and-men-in-stem-often-at-odds-over-workplace-equity/) So, it’s not surprising that Black and Latino STEM workers are [underrepresented in the labor market](https://www.pewresearch.org/science/2021/04/01/stem-jobs-see-uneven-progress-in-increasing-gender-racial-and-ethnic-diversity/). Black people comprise 11% of all workers but only 9% of STEM workers, while Latinos comprise 17% of the total workforce but hold only 8% of STEM jobs.

To better understand STEM attainment for women of color in the last decade, we used publicly available data from the National Center for Education Statistics (NCES) to compare STEM degree attainment with overall degree attainment trends from 2010-2019 [(1)](#1). We focused on doctoral degree attainment, as this is highly correlated with both faculty careers and [careers in research and development (R&D](https://www.bls.gov/ooh/life-physical-and-social-science/home.htm)).

In our findings, we centered the experiences of Black and Latina women in STEM, as they face a [“double-bind” and have been excluded from STEM spaces](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/epdf/10.1080/0194262X.2017.1371658?needAccess=true&role=button). Despite this exclusion, we recognize that Black and Latina women engage in STEM work and have contributed significantly to STEM innovations, which often receive little recognition. By identifying and addressing a need for increased support for women of color in STEM, we can provide a path for more equitable opportunities and outcomes.

### Key Findings

In [our analysis of STEM attainment rates by race/ethnicity and gender](https://public.tableau.com/app/profile/the.education.trust/viz/ExploringWomenofColorDegreeAttainmentinSTEM/STEMDegreeAttainmentTrends), [(2)](#2) we found that there are still persistent inequities in STEM degree attainment for people of color, women, and women of color. We also found that increases in STEM doctoral degrees are not proportional to the rate at which women earn doctoral degrees generally. In all cases, women outpace men in earning associate, bachelor’s, master’s, and doctoral degrees; but in STEM, men within the same race or ethnicity have surpassed women for the last 10 years. Our data suggests that women of color face compounding challenges in STEM pathways.

While no single key finding summarizes the experiences of the individuals whose identities and academic careers are represented in this sample, we have identified several broad trends:

### Data on Black women in STEM

### Data on Latina women in STEM

[Our dashboard](https://public.tableau.com/app/profile/the.education.trust/viz/ExploringWomenofColorDegreeAttainmentinSTEM/STEMDegreeAttainmentTrends) allows users to see various trends grouped by race/ethnicity/gender/degree type. But the data is just one part of picture; as readers engage with it, we ask them to remember that the numbers represent people and their lived experiences.

Without a [nurturing, encouraging academic environment](https://www.emerald.com/insight/content/doi/10.1108/JME-01-2016-0007/full/html), students do not have the same opportunity to thrive as they would with greater support. Many women of color in STEM face a host of barriers, including [challenging campus climates](https://urgeoscience.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/33/2021/03/The-Troubled-Success-of-Black-Women-in-STEM.pdf); racism and sexism, absence of diverse faculty; a [lack of culturally relevant curricula](https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ1154485); and individualistic culture. Without pathways to higher-paying jobs, including STEM careers, Black women and Latinas will continue to earn [disproportionately low salaries](https://www.epi.org/publication/black-and-hispanic-women-are-hit-particularly-hard-by-the-gender-wage-gap/).

Advancing STEM equity also includes combating racism and sexism in higher education and the workplace. STEM exists within these broader systems and is therefore impacted by policies and practices that push women of color out of STEM fields. In an ever advancing and changing world, now more than ever there is a need for diverse voices and innovative thinking. It is through the collaborative and collective efforts of all STEM thinkers and doers that we will be able to solve problems such as climate change and wealth inequality.

### What leaders can do to increase the number of women of color in STEM

The societal and economic benefits of increasing training and education for STEM careers to meet growing labor market demands have been widely acknowledged by many policymakers and leaders.

### Federal

At the federal level, the Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors ([CHIPS](https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/statements-releases/2022/08/09/fact-sheet-chips-and-science-act-will-lower-costs-create-jobs-strengthen-supply-chains-and-counter-china/)) and Science Act of 2022 was developed to “ensure the future is made in ALL of America and unlock opportunities in science and technology for those who have been historically left out,” with the goal of propelling the U.S. to lead the globe in STEM advancement. Additionally, President Joe Biden signed a federal omnibus package aimed at increasing research and development across agencies and specifically highlighted the Institute for Education Sciences (IES). Programs like the U.S. Department of Education’s [Raise the Bar: STEM Excellence for All Students Initiative](https://www.ed.gov/news/press-releases/us-department-education-launches-new-initiative-enhance-stem-education-all-students) and Sec. Miguel Cardona’s recent efforts to promote equity in access to resources and opportunities through discretionary grants acknowledge the growing demand for diversity in STEM.

Additionally, the federal government can improve career pathways in STEM for women of color by investing additional funds in MSIs and community colleges; undergraduate and graduate [research opportunities](https://ncspacegrant.ncsu.edu/higher-ed-opportunities/msi-stem-bridge-scholarship-program/); interventions and initiatives that improve access and success and provide technical assistance for institutions and states working to improve systems and supports for students, such as robust longitudinal data systems and investments in R&D infrastructure.

#### The CHIPS Act

The CHIPS Act of 2022 invests $13.2 billion in R&D and workforce development, increasing the number of graduates and workers in STEM fields over time and provides resources funding for training and educational opportunities starting in K-12 and continuing through graduate-level education. It also aims to expand access to STEM by:

### State

At the state level, leaders and advocacy groups are investigating ways to create better alignment with state and local employment needs. For example, [Washington STEM](https://washingtonstem.org/focus-areas/), an education nonprofit organization based in Seattle, WA, is helping state lawmakers craft policies that remove barriers and create more pathways to address STEM education issues, such as the underrepresentation of certain student groups, including women of color.

We encourage more state leaders to invest in [college and career readiness](https://west.edtrust.org/college-and-career-readiness/) and [increase access to AP coursework](https://www.americanprogress.org/article/closing-advanced-coursework-equity-gaps-students/) and need-based scholarship programs to better prepare more students for STEM education and career pathways. We also urge state leaders to increase support for evidence-based student success initiatives, remove barriers for transfer and articulation between colleges, and target funding to institutions that serve the most Black and Latino students and students from low-income families.

### Institutions of Higher Education

At the institutional level, colleges can expand STEM pathways for Black women and Latinas by increasing the representation of diverse faculty and improving [campus racial climates](/the-equity-line/improving-on-campus-racial-climate/), which has [proven positive effects](https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2012-07685-010) on outcomes for students and faculty, particularly for women and students of color in STEM. Additionally, colleges can partner with organizations such as the National Society of Black Engineers (NSBE) and Society of Hispanic Professional Engineers (SHPE) to provide academic support, community, and professional development for Black and Latino students pursuing STEM. Institutions can also seek out technical assistance opportunities to increase [funding for research and programming](https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/msrdc-science-and-technology-research-partnership) and provide technical assistance at campuses serving the most Black and Latino students.

We know [faculty diversity and student success are interrelated](/resource/faculty-diversity-and-student-success-go-hand-in-hand-so-why-are-university-faculties-so-white/) and that the former can have a positive impact on the latter; so it stands to reason that increasing the diversity of faculty in STEM would help increase the number of students of color pursuing STEM degrees and careers. Closing the gap in access to higher education is a crucial step toward creating a more just and equitable society. And investing in the success of students of color is vital for the future economic competitiveness and social cohesion of our country. Empowering more women of color to pursue and thrive in STEM careers would help create a dynamic and innovative workforce that can advance the country’s competitiveness in a technology-driven global economy.

### Notes

### About the Authors

**Brianna Wright** is a doctoral student in higher education and organizational change at the University of California, Los Angeles, and a former intern at The Education Trust.

**Olivia Gunther** is a recent graduate of the master of public policy program at the University of Chicago and a former higher education data/research intern at The Education Trust.

**Jinann Bitar** is director of higher education research & data analytics at The Education Trust.

*NOTE: Readers should consider the identities of the primary authors when engaging with the data in this blog and also note that the authors are represented in this data in various ways. For example, Briana Wright is Afro-Latina and Olivia Gunther is White. Both are women who’ve taken STEM courses and use quantitative methods and data analytics in their professional work, and both have encountered challenges in academic environments dominated by people who do not look like them, but they experience the world differently on account of their different intersecting identities. No two people’s experiences and positionality are alike, so we can only create more equity in STEM by paying attention to multiple perspectives and recognizing that women of color are significantly underrepresented in STEM spaces.*

--- SOURCE 2: Equity in PreK 12 STEM Education - National Academies ---
URL: https://www.nationalacademies.org/our-work/equity-in-prek-12-stem-education

SUMMARY:
Equity in PreK 12 STEM Education | National Academies The Board on Science Education (BOSE) with the National Academy of Engineering (NAE) are supporting a project that provides practical, evidence-based guidance on how to support educational equity in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) education at all levels of the PreK-12 system. By bringing the rigor of a National Academies’ consensus study process to this critical issue, the project will provide actionable recommendations for education policy and program stakeholders. Based on peer-reviewed and grey literature, commissioned papers, input from the field engagements, and committee deliberations, the committee will write a consensus report that discusses how systemic inequity in STEM education can be addressed at all levels of the PreK-12 system to promote success in STEM for all students, regardless of background, demographic status, and community.

The Board on Science Education (BOSE) with the National Academy of Engineering (NAE) are supporting a project that provides practical, evidence-based guidance on how to support educational equity in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) education at all levels of the PreK-12 system. By bringing the rigor of a National Academies’ consensus study process to this critical issue, the project will provide actionable recommendations for education policy and program stakeholders.

[### Equity in K-12 STEM Education: Framing Decisions for the Future](https://nap.nationalacademies.org/26859)

Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) live in the American imagination as promising tools for solving pressing global challenges and enhancing quality of life. Despite the importance of the STEM disciplines in the landscape of U.S. political, economic, and social priorities, STEM learning opportunities are unevenly distributed, and the experiences an individual has in STEM education are likely to vary tremendously based on their race, ethnicity, socio-economic class, gender, and a myriad of other factors.

Equity in K-12 STEM Education: Framing Decisions for the Future approaches equity in STEM education not as a singular goal but as an ongoing process that requires intentional decision-making and action toward addressing and disrupting existing inequities and envisioning a more just future. Stakeholders at all levels of the education system - including state, district, and school leaders and classroom teachers - have roles as decision-makers who can advance equity. This consensus study report provides five equity frames as a guide to help decision-makers articulate short- and long-term goals for equity and make decisions about policy and practice.

Description
-----------

 An ad-hoc committee of experts will examine the evidence base related to educational equity and STEM education, plan a series of regional field engagement sessions, and develop recommendations and a research agenda for the field. Based on peer-reviewed and grey literature, commissioned papers, input from the field engagements, and committee deliberations, the committee will write a consensus report that discusses how systemic inequity in STEM education can be addressed at all levels of the PreK-12 system to promote success in STEM for all students, regardless of background, demographic status, and community.

 The committee will:

*   Identify and describe inequity in the state of PreK-12 STEM education in the United States, and examine the evidence on explanations of and interventions to address those inequities.
*   Consider how ongoing implementation of state-level science and mathematics standards can take into account and address existing inequities in such areas as resource and human capital distribution, course offerings, instructional approaches (including classroom management and disciplinary policy), family and community engagement, enrichment programs, access to technology and other concerns relevant to persistent inequalities reflected in the literature.
*   Review evidence on policy and program interventions at the federal, state, district, and classroom levels that have addressed equity concerns and might be considered promising practices
*   Develop recommendations for policy, practice and research to promote success for all students in PreK-12 STEM learning.

Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education Why STEM? Science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) represent the leading sector for jobs and economic growth in the United States through 2027. According to the Education Commission of the States (2020), U.S. STEM jobs will experience thirteen percent growth in the coming decade where all other job sectors combined will grow by nine percent. At issue is the need for a larger STEM-proficient workforce to strengthen our economy and make the United States more competitive internationally. Yet U.S. business leaders report difficulty finding available STEM-proficient workers (Education Commission of the States, 2020). Although the United States has made recent strides in strengthening STEM education, students of color and girls still lag behind. To meet the growing demand for our 21st century workforce, Pre-K-12 educators in the United States must accelerate efforts to use STEM-leaning principles in their teaching. Bridging diversity and gender gaps also requires increased access for all students in the pursuit of education and careers in STEM fields. STEM education is a fundamental tool for improving people’s lives and strengthening their engagement in our complex and increasingly global society. A basic understanding of STEM principles allows students to think critically, understand the world around them, and become contributing, productive members of society. Schooling in general, and STEM education in particular, provides important learning opportunities that develop critical thinking skills and broaden understanding. All young people can benefit substantially from increased access to quality STEM education and careers. Students of color, however, historically have not had access to high quality STEM teachers or education, and continue to be underrepresented in STEM classes and careers (Education Commission of the States, 2020). Teacher training, increased access to materials and technology at urban and rural schools, and a concerted effort to propel girls and students of color toward degrees in STEM – from an early age – will be the benchmarks of our Nation’s success. Educating our students to meet the economic demands of the 21st century will necessitate an expansion of our perceptions of who will represent our future leaders and workers in the STEM fields and will require an unprecedented push to provide access to historically underrepresented groups. Introduction 2 Advancing Equity through STEM Education Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved.
The Context for STEM Education Challenges remain to providing equitable education for all students. Students and teachers of color are significantly underrepresented in STEM education. In addition, considerable achievement gaps continue to exist between white students and students of racially, linguistically, and culturally diverse backgrounds. The problems related to equity and STEM can be tracked historically to a Western perception of science and math. Racial and gender stereotypes have permeated current educational and social conditions. The implicit perceptions artificially limiting who can do math and science remain. The education system reflects the inequities that exist in society at large, and also tends to reinforce these inequities (Bowles & Gintis, 2002). While political and economic changes necessary for equity in education may go beyond the purview of individual schools and teachers, there are school-level policies and classroom practices that can positively influence STEM student outcomes. This guide promotes efforts to create more STEM-equitable learning environments and outcomes for all students.
Education and Schooling The effective inclusion of girls and children of color was absent in the design of 19th century public education. Further, for the majority of White Americans, public education was designed primarily for the purpose of training workers for the factory assembly line. As education became more widely accessible, presenting a means for escaping economic hardship and achieving “the American Dream” unfortunately became increasingly stratified. High quality education was generally reserved for those of higher economic or social status, or both. Equity and opportunity in education have expanded significantly since then. For example, the civil rights era of the mid-20th century saw the desegregation of southern schools; in addition to increased college access and more equitable education rights for girls, people with disabilities (through the Rehabilitation Act), Emerging Multilingual Learners (EML, often called “English Learners”), and African Americans. Yet even with civil rights legislation and increased enforcement of related laws; issues of poverty, racism, and sexism that exist in society at large continue to impact educational outcomes.
STEM Just as Western European culture has held a privileged status in American schools, it has also dominated the supposedly value-neutral realms of science, mathematics, technology, and engineering. Like the education system, Western STEM education and career opportunity have a somewhat checkered past, at times both reflecting and reinforcing the inequality of society as a whole. Historically, the STEM disciplines have been dominated by White men. The contributions of women and people of color have been marginalized throughout history. One notable example is in the case of physicist, Lise Meitner, who, with Otto Hahn and their research group, discovered nuclear fission. Only Hahn was awarded a Nobel Prize (in 1944) for the discovery. Less well known is the fact that current STEM research often does not include patterns of thinking and communicating that are common in non-dominant cultures; patterns that could be beneficial to research aimed at benefitting everyone in society. Even in today’s active learning classrooms, students often are not afforded the chance to decide what questions to pursue and how to pursue them: essentially how to be active in their learning. This reinforces the idea that fluency in STEM is something that must be transmitted from others. When enacted, this notion results in the dismissal of ideas, not only for students who bring non-Western cultures and values to the classroom, but for all students who can benefit from more intellectually autonomous experiences in the K-12 classroom. 3 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education Each principle is addressed by a set of key features that expand and describe how to use the principles when designing and delivering instruction. The table below outlines these principles and their corresponding key features. How to Use This Guide This guide provides principles and features that are necessary for improving equity in STEM education. Rather than a stand-alone resource, it is designed as a tool to be used in conjunction with existing STEM curricula to foster learning within our growing racially, culturally, and linguistically diverse populations. The guide consists of five principles that highlight the central elements of our equity approach to STEM: 1. STEM education is a process for building knowledge about our world and universe.
2. Culturally responsive STEM instruction yields positive results.
3. Developmentally appropriate STEM instruction enriches the learning process and promotes academic achievement.
4. STEM knowledge and perspectives are for everyone.
5. STEM education should address barriers to learning so that all students experience the power of the learning process for improving their lives and the lives of others.
PART 1 Principles 6 Advancing Equity through STEM Education Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved.
Principles This guide is organized to provide educators with information needed to implement our STEM approach in the classroom. Each principle provides a rationale and summary of relevant research findings that support its importance for providing STEM instruction and assessment from an equity perspective. Following each principle, a description of key features and K-12 classroom examples are provided. The activities can be used as is, or adapted for different grade levels. The guide can be used as a trainer-of-trainer model, a tool to be used by teachers in their classrooms, or both.
The foundation of this guide is a problem-based learning (PBL) pedagogy. Using this method teachers present complex and real-world problems to inspire student learning. Rather than simply delivering content through direct instruction, teachers promote guided, student-driven problem-solving through which students develop understanding of curricular concepts and principles. PBL promotes life-long learning through the development of critical thinking skills, communication skills, and problem-solving abilities, while providing opportunities to work in groups to find and evaluate research materials (Duch, et al., 2001). This approach is easily contextualized within all disciplines, particularly the four STEM disciplines. 7 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Understanding by Design Throughout this guide, we present each classroom-focused strategy and instructional idea using the Understanding by Design (UbD) Framework (Association for Supervision and Curriculum Development [ASCD], 2012). UbD is a deliberative instructional model that supports equitable teaching and learning in all classrooms. The UbD Framework focuses teaching strategies on the “big ideas” important in instruction. When utilizing the UbD Framework, teachers are empowered to think intentionally about planning curriculum without offering a prescriptive plan. UbD supports teachers in long-term backward planning; allowing them to focus first on desired results from instruction, then student assessment evidence, and finally specific learning experiences and instruction. Teachers become “coaches of understanding,” helping students gain meaning from instruction. The UbD Framework focuses on the continual improvement of all students’ learning: It supports equitable teaching and learning through the development of deep and individualized student understanding. Following the Understanding by Design Framework: Stage 1: Identify desired results (essential questions), Stage 2: Determine assessment evidence (assessment of performance indicator), and Stage 3: Plan learning experiences and instruction (learning plan), (Wiggins and McTighe, 2005). Each instructional suggestion will be presented in the following format: • Lesson • Content Areas(s) • Step One: Essential Questions • Step Two: Assessment of Performance Indicator • Step Three: Learning Plan 11 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education The most important aspect to keep in mind while teaching STEM is that it is not actually a collection of facts and skills. STEM is a process that connects disciplines so that students can understand the interconnections between science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. STEM learning is a process of understanding that allows students to design and propose solutions to problems that are contextualized, authentic, and meaningful (Chalfour, 2010). Through the use of the scientific method which exists in multiple forms, important elements can be learned from attempts to gain new knowledge or problem-solve. These include using logic and careful observations of the natural world in addition to brainstorming, using intuition, finding analogies, communicating in groups, and constructing arguments (Cole, 2008). These are all tools that scientists, mathematicians, and engineers use in the service of making sense of a particular question or phenomenon. In 1936, Albert Einstein wrote, “The whole of science is nothing more than a refinement of everyday thinking.” He believed scientists should not confine their critical thinking to their respective fields but should include connections to the real world; they should be philosophers as well as scientists. By gathering relationships between concepts, scientists can organize the world (Einstein, pp. 22-25).
First, STEM is everywhere, flows from everyday thinking, and should include everyone. In daily weather forecasts (science), handheld devices (technology), buildings we live and work in (engineering), money we use to pay for goods and groceries (math), STEM is everywhere and for everyone.
Second, everyday thinking must then be refined in a way that makes sense, so that conclusions can be drawn and decisions made. Effective STEM learning involves constantly making decisions based on evidence. Like STEM professionals, learners have to decide what questions to ask, what data to collect, the best way to collect that data, what the results mean, how the results fit (or do not fit) with other things they think they know, and whether to consult another expert. This approach leads to the scientific method that everyone can internalize and use to understand and influence the world around them. Principle One 01 The whole of science is nothing more than a refinement of everyday thinking.
- Albert Einstein STEM education is a process for building knowledge about our world and universe.
12 Advancing Equity through STEM Education Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved.
Third, effective STEM learning is a collaborative and collective process. STEM practice is a social endeavor, as experts communicate not only their findings with each other, but also their methods, thoughts, and ideas. There are multiple cycles of evidence-based iteration throughout the process, which are not done in isolation. It must be noted that getting the best answer to any particular question in STEM comes only at the end of this process. Unfortunately, because tests often measure answers only, the process is passed over or deemphasized. Yet it is the process that is the core of STEM thinking. For example, most students will be better served if they know how to design and conduct an investigation than if they know the speed of light or what various types of bears eat.
Fourth, STEM has a positive impact on students’ means of understanding: There is growing recognition that students’ attitudes, beliefs about disciplinary knowledge, and patterns of choosing certain learning approaches over others play an important role in learning. These attitudes, beliefs, and behaviors are often called epistemologies, and they are not always consciously held. Students who are most successful in a discipline understand the nature of the discipline: how its canonical knowledge is constructed, how to monitor and improve their own learning, and how to explore— from disciplinary evidence—the world and their own lives. Many experts believe that improving students’ epistemological views and behaviors is an important goal; others see it primarily as a means to improve conceptual understanding and problem-solving skills. Regardless of the reason, it is clear that these skills are essential for effective learning. Fifth, STEM educators should use students’ background knowledge and ideas about STEM: Addressing students’ views of the nature of STEM and of learning is something that should occur in tandem with the teaching of content and practices. Special attention should be paid to these views (or to behavior that may indicate certain views), both when planning a lesson and when implementing and assessing it. Students bring to the classroom a range of ideas about knowledge and learning that may come from their home cultures, day-to-day lives, or both. For example, they know that sometimes knowledge is constructed logically (“I figured it out”), invented (“I chose my pet’s name”), and communicated from others (“the teacher told me”). These views are not scientifically right or wrong, but are ways of approaching learning that teachers can use to support more in-depth exploration about content and processes of STEM disciplines.
Sixth, STEM educators should encourage students to reflect on their learning process. STEM learning is best accomplished when students have the opportunity to explicitly reflect on the learning process. Shifting instructional practices to better support the STEM process of building knowledge may indeed lead to significant improvement in students’ understanding of STEM concepts, but it is not sufficient. Reflection helps develop greater understanding of the process itself and the ability to apply it in productive ways.
A key aspect of STEM education is the emphasis on the ability to solve problems. When STEM education begins at an early age, students develop important skills (e.g. communication, collaboration, inquiry, problem solving, and flexibility) that form the foundation for their education (Next Generation Science Standards [NGSS] Lead States, 2013).
13 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education STEM learning as a process of knowing that helps students become problem solvers As described earlier, STEM learning is a process that extends beyond STEM disciplines. An example of a pedagogical approach that supports the inquiry nature of STEM learning is a problem-based teaching and learning approach. Problem-based teaching and learning takes place when “students encounter carefully selected, but ill-structured problems before they experience any instruction in the particular focus area” (Bridges and Hallinger, 1992, pp. 5-6). The uniqueness of ill-structured problems is their open-endedness. Rather than formulating a question precisely that channels students to a certain end or solution, ill-structured problems support flexible STEM-focused thinking. Students must identify what they actually consider the problem to be, what solution they may devise, and then reflect on a process by which to get there. The literature often confuses the term problem-based learning with project-based learning, inquiry-based learning, expeditionary learning, and other terms. While they all share some characteristics, the unique feature of problem-based learning includes a flexible process of inquiry rather than in a more constrained discipline-- focused outcome. An ill-structured problem is one that possesses elements that are unknown and requires multiple solution paths. Students guided by personal opinion, beliefs, or both, are required to examine the evidence from multiple perspectives and use it to arrive at a defensible outcome (Kain, 2003). Although, there is no strict protocol for what problem-based learning must look like, in a K-12 classroom an evidence-based problem-solving approach should include the following (Parker et al., 2017): • Identification of an everyday problem that can be explored from multiple disciplinary perspectives.
• Brainstorming of potential solutions with other learners.
• Completion of background research to inform (from existing literature) what is known about the everyday problem or dilemma.
• Development of a model to help “solve” the problem or dilemma.
• Testing of the proposed model.
• Reflection on the results of the proposed model.
• Improvement on the proposed model.
• Further reflection and improvement until the model is optimized. Incorporating problem-based learning into the K-12 setting supports students through an evidence-based approach while connecting necessary, subject-based knowledge that can include science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. An example of a problem-based learning topic suitable for grades K-12 is scarcity of clean, potable water. In the developed world we take for granted the availability of water. In less developed areas of the world, millions of people lack access to clean, safe water. Further, in rural areas, women and girls often bear the burden of walking miles at a time to gather water from streams and ponds—streams and ponds that may be full of water-borne bacteria, making them and their families sick. Water is the foundation of life. And still today, around the world far too many people suffer from water scarcity.
Water scarcity, and possible solutions to this problem, is a topic that can be studied from a myriad of perspectives in the K-12 classroom and can incorporate, not only the STEM subjects, but social studies, English, foreign language, and the arts. A good course to better understand water scarcity and how it can be applied in the K-12 classroom can be found in the work of The Water Project (2007). What follows is a specific example of a middle school classroom lesson written in the UbD framework.
Key Feature One 14 Advancing Equity through STEM Education Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved.
Performance Indicators • Students will explore, through a problem-based learning approach, how they can help various populations around the world enhance the amount of water available to them. • Students will gain knowledge about the science and social issues facing water purification. • Students will collaborate, research, and share findings about filtration systems from around the world.
• The desired outcome is a workable, affordable, and easily maintainable water purification prototype. Step One: Essential Question How can students assist populations around the world to enhance the availability of potable (drinkable) water by designing cost-effective water filters? Step Two: Assessment of Performance Indicators • A research paper of appropriate length about the worldwide scarcity of potable water. • An engineering notebook describing the process of building the water filter prototype.
• A workable, affordable, and easily maintainable water purification prototype.
Step Three: Learning Plan Lesson plans for this investigation are ubiquitous on the Internet. Two suggested lesson plans for the investigation include: • The Water Project: https://thewaterproject.org/resources/lesson-plans/ • NASA: https://www.nasa.gov/offices/education/programs/national/summer/education_resources/ engineering_grades7-9/E_water-filtration.html Both of the suggested lesson plans align with Key Feature One by framing the problem of water scarcity while approaching the problem from multiple perspectives. Both lessons ask students to apply their own knowledge of the world while researching new knowledge. Through both lessons, students collaborate, research, and share findings; developing new solutions to our world’s water scarcity problem.

Advancing Equity through STEM Education Across all disciplines, culturally responsive pedagogy is grounded in instruction that relates course content and practices to the culture that each student brings to the classroom (Ladson-Billings, 2014). Historically, the term culturally responsive teaching began with African American students in the United States, but recent research has found it to be an effective educational approach with other groups of students (Dee & Penner, 2017; Matthews & López, 2018). A culturally responsive STEM education approach seeks to combine students’ diverse cultural backgrounds, knowledge, and practices with the world of STEM. This is especially important in elementary school when students are developing basic functional skills such as reading, writing, and mathematics. STEM instruction in the K-12 classroom can be tailored to bridge the world of a student’s culture, the school, and STEM. For example, Dr. Julie Brown (2015), assistant professor at the College of Education, University of Minnesota discusses a “hybrid knowledge” model for culturally responsive teaching she developed to tap into the lived experiences of students and their families. Brown suggests that teachers learn from students and their families as much as imparting new knowledge to students. Brown provides an example of how this concept can be used in the classroom. In Minneapolis, seventh grade science students learned how the immune system protects against microscopic organisms (e.g., bacteria and fungi) which infect the human body and affect oral health. Minneapolis is home to a large population of Somali immigrants. Therefore, this particular lesson was tailored to the Somali students in the classroom. In the Somali culture, oral hygiene does not necessarily involve brushing teeth with a toothbrush or using toothpaste and mouthwash. In the Somali community, practices that date back centuries are used which are the source of great cultural pride. It is common to use a miswak chewing stick five times a day in advance of Islamic prayers. Charcoal is also used as a whitening and cleaning agent. Scientific research documents that the use of the miswak effectively removes plaque and inhibits periodontal disease. The scientific principles behind miswak and charcoal use for oral hygiene can be an interesting exploratory topic for a science lesson. Incorporating cultural scientific knowledge stimulates student learning, enables teachers to develop an improved relationship with the students and families, and reduces the achievement gap in STEM education. As seen in the case with Somali students, a culturally responsive approach to instruction stimulates learning by building new knowledge in a familiar context, which encourages students to further study STEM subjects. enriched_points.expanded_content: Persistent gaps in STEM degree attainment exist, particularly for women of color, highlighting systemic barriers and the need for targeted interventions to improve equity.

Ongoing efforts are essential to dismantle inequities in STEM education, ensuring diverse representation and equitable access for historically marginalized groups, especially women and people of color.

enriched_points.point_title: Vision for the Future of STEM Education Equity
enriched_points.expanded_content: A future where all students, regardless of race or gender, thrive in STEM fields, supported by inclusive policies, diverse role models, and equitable educational practices.
